title: Getting Started
description: Installation, setup, and your first Elle program

content.text: Elle is written in Rust and requires `cargo` to build. Clone the repository and build the interpreter:

git clone https://github.com/elle-lisp/elle.git
cd elle
cargo build --release
The compiled binary will be at `./target/release/elle`. You can add it to your PATH or use it directly.
Create a file `hello.lisp` with your Elle code:

(print "Hello, Elle!")
(println)
Run it with:
./target/release/elle hello.lisp
You can also make scripts executable with a shebang:
Let's write a program that demonstrates a few core features. Create `greet.lisp`:

# A greeting program
(def name "World")
(def greeting (-> "Hello, " (append name) (append "!")))
(println greeting)
Run it:
./target/release/elle greet.lisp
Output:
Hello, World!
Elle uses S-expressions with prefix notation. Comments start with `#`.
**Variables and Functions**

# immutable binding
(def x 42)

# named function (shorthand for def + fn)
(defn add [a b] (+ a b))

# anonymous function
(def double (fn [x] (* x 2)))

(println (add x 8))    # 50
(println (double x))   # 84
**Conditionals**

(if (> 10 5)
  (println "10 is greater than 5")
  (println "10 is not greater than 5"))
**Local Bindings**

# let creates local variables
(let ((x 10)
      (y 20))
  (println (+ x y)))
**Collections**

# arrays
(def nums [1 2 3 4 5])
(each n in nums (print n " "))
(println)

# structs
(def point {:x 10 :y 20})
(println (get point :x))  # 10
Now that you've got Elle running, explore:

items: [Language Guide](language-guide.html) — data types, functions, control flow, pattern matching
[Examples](examples.html) — 20+ runnable programs covering every major feature
